what_it_does: An acceptance-test script that exercises three red-team hardening remediations: F2 (a self-apply whose git commit fails must roll back and raise), F4 (a twin's own output must never become training signal, even if laundered back as a user turn), and F6 (a malformed panel file must emit a warning event instead of being silently dropped). It defines a small check helper and runs three temp-directory scenarios, then prints a pass count.
observations: Top docstring maps each test block to a named remediation (F2, F4, F6) and notes where other gates are covered.
Uses a global PASS counter and a hand-rolled check(label, cond) helper rather than a test framework.
F2 creates a non-git nodes directory and expects apply_node to raise RuntimeError and leave no file behind.
F4 appends a genuine operator turn, a twin assistant turn, and then the twin text replayed as a user turn, then checks training_signal().
F6 writes invalid JSON to a panel file and checks that recent_events contains a warning referencing broken.json.
Each scenario creates its own tempfile work directory and cleans it up in a finally block.
standouts: The echo-guard / model-collapse prevention test (F4) is the most domain-specific signal.
Fail-loud rollback requirement is tested by absence of the applied file, not just by exception.
Malformed-panel fallback is verified by an emitted warning event rather than a returned error value.
conventions: Follows the repo pattern of inserting ROOT into sys.path and importing store/runtime modules.
Uses tempfile + shutil.rmtree for isolated test fixtures.
Breaks typical unittest/pytest conventions by using a global counter and assert inside a helper.
concerns: F2 only catches RuntimeError, so a different exception type would be reported as failure but not as 'raised', potentially masking the real error.
The rollback check relies on file absence, which could pass if the file was never written for unrelated reasons.
Global mutable PASS is not thread-safe and makes the script order-dependent.
Hard-coded fixture strings and magic numbers (999, 5) reduce readability.
notes: A red-team acceptance script that turns three safety properties into concrete, file-system-level assertions.
questions: What does Suite.apply_node do when git is unavailable, and how does it perform the rollback?
What fields does training_signal() return, and how does it detect a laundered twin turn?
What event schema does store.recent_events use, and what guarantees list_panels makes about malformed files?
What do the default and resolved parameters to surface mean?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it tests F2, F4, and F6 with isolated scenarios.
intention_signals: The file is trying to prove that critical safety remediations are observable and enforced at runtime, especially around destructive changes, data lineage, and error visibility.
novelty: Tests are framed as red-team remediations with short mnemonic codes (F2/F4/F6) and include an adversarial role-laundering scenario.
contribution: Provides executable acceptance criteria for three hardening properties and a lightweight check harness that can be run as a standalone script.
summary_for_embedding: Standalone acceptance test script that verifies F2 git-fail rollback, F4 twin-output exclusion from training signal even when role-laundered, and F6 warning emission for malformed panel files.
intention_for_embedding: Validate red-team hardening remediations by exercising edge cases that could otherwise silently compromise safety.

symbols.does: Assertion helper that increments a global pass counter and prints an ok message.
symbols.description: check(label, cond) asserts cond with a labeled failure message, increments the global PASS counter on success, and prints 'ok <label>'. It is used throughout the script to record acceptance-criteria results.
